text: Higher fat mass index (FMI) is associated with lower running speed in both women and men.
In men, high fat-free mass index (FFMI > 20 kg/m²) is associated with poor running performance.
In women, moderate fat-free mass index (FFMI 15–16.4 kg/m²) is associated with higher running speed compared to lower FFMI.
Body composition (FMI and FFMI) predicts running speed better than BMI alone, explaining roughly twice the variance in women.
BMI is negatively associated with running speed in both sexes, but it conflates fat mass and lean mass effects that act in opposite directions.
Excess muscle mass beyond a threshold can impair running performance in men, suggesting an optimal lean mass range for distance running.
